title: Stem cells — cells that can become anything
examTip: Know the difference between embryonic and adult stem cells, be able to describe a therapeutic use, and be ready to discuss the ethical arguments for and against — these come up as 6-mark questions.
introTips: This lesson explains one of the most powerful ideas in biology — that some cells haven't yet decided what they're going to be.
You'll need to explain what makes embryonic stem cells different from adult stem cells, and give real examples of therapeutic uses.
AQA loves asking you to evaluate the ethical debate — so you need arguments on both sides, not just one.
intro: Every cell in your body started as one single cell. At some point, each of those cells 'switched on' a set of genes and became something specific — a nerve cell, a muscle cell, a blood cell. Stem cells are the ones that haven't done that yet. They can still become almost anything. That's extraordinary — and it's why scientists are so interested in them.

keyPoints.heading: What stem cells are
keyPoints.content: A stem cell is an undifferentiated cell — it hasn't specialised yet. Stem cells can divide by mitosis to produce more stem cells, and they can differentiate (change) into specialised cell types. This combination — divide AND differentiate — is what makes them unique. Most cells in your body have already differentiated and can only make copies of themselves.
writeBullets: Stem cell = undifferentiated cell that has not yet specialised
Can divide to produce more stem cells (self-renewal)
Can differentiate into specialised cell types
Differentiation = switching on specific genes to become a particular cell type

keyTerms.def: An undifferentiated cell capable of dividing and differentiating into specialised cell types
The process by which a cell develops a specialised structure and function by switching on specific genes

keyPoints.memTip: Two things to remember: stem cells can DIVIDE and DIFFERENTIATE. If a question asks what's special about them, give both — just dividing isn't enough; so is just differentiating. Use 'D and D' as your hook.

keyPoints.heading: Embryonic vs adult stem cells
keyPoints.content: Embryonic stem cells are found in early embryos. They are pluripotent — they can become any cell type in the body. Adult stem cells are found in specific tissues (e.g. bone marrow). They are multipotent — they can only differentiate into a limited range of cell types. For example, bone marrow stem cells make red blood cells, white blood cells and platelets — but not nerve or muscle cells. Plants have their own stem cells called meristems, found at the tips of roots and shoots.
writeBullets: Embryonic stem cells = pluripotent (can become ANY cell type)
Adult stem cells = multipotent (limited range — only certain cell types)
Bone marrow stem cells make blood cells: red, white, and platelets
Plant stem cells = meristems — found at root and shoot tips
Meristem cells are used in cloning plants and tissue culture

keyTerms.def: Able to differentiate into any type of cell in the body (embryonic stem cells)
Able to differentiate into a limited range of cell types (adult stem cells)
Plant stem cell tissue found at the tips of roots and shoots, capable of producing new plant cells

keyPoints.memTip: PLURIpotent = PLURal possibilities = ANY cell type. MULTIpotent = MULTIPLE but not all = limited range. The 'pluri' vs 'multi' distinction is what AQA tests — get that pair sharp.

keyPoints.heading: Therapeutic uses of stem cells
keyPoints.content: Because stem cells can become specialised cells, they could be used to replace damaged or diseased tissue. This is called therapeutic cloning or stem cell therapy. The most established use is treating leukaemia — a cancer of the blood — using a bone marrow transplant. The donor's adult stem cells replace the patient's faulty blood-producing cells. Future potential uses include treating Parkinson's disease (replacing damaged nerve cells), type 1 diabetes (replacing insulin-producing cells), and paralysis (repairing damaged spinal cord tissue).
writeBullets: Therapeutic use = using stem cells to replace damaged or diseased tissue
Established use: bone marrow transplant to treat leukaemia
Potential future uses: Parkinson's disease, type 1 diabetes, paralysis/spinal cord damage
Adult stem cells from donor bone marrow replace patient's faulty blood stem cells
Embryonic stem cells offer wider potential because they are pluripotent

keyTerms.def: Using stem cells to treat or cure disease by replacing damaged or non-functioning cells
A treatment for leukaemia in which adult stem cells from a donor's bone marrow replace the patient's faulty blood-producing cells

keyPoints.memTip: For the exam, always give a specific named disease — 'leukaemia' not just 'blood cancer'; 'Parkinson's disease' not just 'a brain condition'. Have three conditions ready: Leukaemia (now), Parkinson's, Diabetes (potential). LPD — Learn, Practise, Do.

keyPoints.heading: The ethical debate
keyPoints.content: Using embryonic stem cells means destroying a human embryo. This raises serious ethical questions. Arguments against: the embryo is a potential human life; destroying it is morally wrong regardless of the benefit. Arguments for: embryos used are typically spare IVF embryos that would otherwise be discarded; the potential to cure serious disease outweighs the cost. Some people suggest using adult stem cells or meristems (plant stem cells) as ethical alternatives — though these have limitations. Scientists are also researching induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) — adult cells reprogrammed to behave like embryonic stem cells.
writeBullets: Embryonic stem cell research requires destroying an embryo
Against: embryo is a potential human life; destruction is ethically wrong
For: spare IVF embryos would be discarded anyway; cure benefit is huge
Alternative: adult stem cells (no embryo destroyed, but more limited)
Alternative: meristems from plants — useful for crop research, no ethical issue
iPSCs: adult cells reprogrammed to act like embryonic stem cells — promising area

keyTerms.def: The moral argument about whether embryonic stem cell research is acceptable, weighing human benefit against the status of the embryo
Plant tissue containing stem cells at root and shoot tips — an ethically uncontroversial alternative to embryonic stem cells

keyPoints.memTip: For a 6-mark evaluate question you need BOTH sides. Write FOR and AGAINST as headings, then give two points each. Finish with a balanced conclusion: 'Overall, given that spare IVF embryos would otherwise be discarded, most scientists argue the potential to cure disease justifies the research.' That structure reliably earns full marks.

1.question: What does it mean to say a stem cell is 'undifferentiated'?
options: It has already become a specialised cell type
It has not yet developed a specialised structure or function
It can only be found in embryos
1.correctFeedback: Exactly — undifferentiated means it hasn't specialised yet. That's what gives stem cells their potential.
1.wrongFeedback: Undifferentiated means the cell hasn't taken on a specific structure or function yet — it's still 'open'. Differentiation is the process of becoming specialised.

3.question: Which of the following best describes the current therapeutic use of stem cells?
options: Using embryonic stem cells to cure Parkinson's disease
Using bone marrow stem cells to treat leukaemia
Reprogramming plant meristems to replace human tissue
Using iPSCs to regenerate spinal cord injury in clinical practice
3.correctFeedback: Right — bone marrow transplant to treat leukaemia is the established, current use. The others are potential future applications.
3.wrongFeedback: The established clinical use right now is bone marrow transplant for leukaemia. Parkinson's, spinal cord repair and iPSCs are areas of active research but not yet routine treatments.

commonMistakes: Saying 'stem cells can become any cell' without specifying — only embryonic (pluripotent) stem cells can do this; adult stem cells are limited.
Confusing 'pluripotent' and 'multipotent' — pluripotent means ANY cell type; multipotent means a limited range.
Forgetting meristems — plant stem cells at root and shoot tips are a common exam bonus point and are relevant to the ethics question.
Only giving one side of the ethical argument — AQA wants both for and against; a one-sided answer cannot score full marks.
Saying leukaemia is treated with embryonic stem cells — it's treated with adult stem cells (bone marrow transplant).
examTips: For any 'evaluate' question on stem cells you must give arguments on both sides — and end with a justified conclusion. Missing one side loses marks immediately.
Always use the correct vocabulary: 'undifferentiated', 'differentiate', 'pluripotent', 'multipotent'. Vague answers like 'they can turn into things' won't score.
When asked about therapeutic uses, name a specific condition — leukaemia for the current use; Parkinson's or diabetes for potential future uses.
revisionCardBullets: Stem cell = undifferentiated cell — can divide and differentiate into specialised cells
Embryonic stem cells = pluripotent (can become ANY cell type)
Adult stem cells = multipotent (limited range — e.g. bone marrow makes blood cells only)
Plant stem cells = meristems, at root tips and shoot tips
Current therapeutic use: bone marrow transplant treats leukaemia
Potential future uses: Parkinson's disease, type 1 diabetes, paralysis
Ethical argument against: embryo is a potential life; destroying it is wrong
Ethical argument for: spare IVF embryos would be discarded; benefit is huge
iPSCs = adult cells reprogrammed to act like embryonic stem cells — avoids ethical issues
